title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered as neighbourhood sports coaches. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Daniel, The Junior Football Coach
Daniel started coaching under-tens football after his son joined the team and the club needed another adult. [47]He soon realised that arranging fair substitutions was harder than teaching passing, because every child counted the minutes they spent on the pitch.[/47] His job as a project manager helped him create a rotation chart that parents could see before matches began. [48]The chart, printed in large letters, reduced arguments on the touchline and let him focus on encouraging players who were nervous about making mistakes.[/48] Daniel had expected competition to motivate the children, but some improved most during playful drills where nobody kept score. His proudest moment was when the strongest player passed to a newcomer instead of trying to score alone. He now says coaching children means designing fairness carefully, not simply shouting instructions. He carries spare shoelaces, because small practical problems can become big emotional ones just before kick-off on a cold morning.
texts.B:
### Aisha, The Running Group Leader
Aisha founded a beginners' running group after recovering from an injury that had made her afraid of exercise and embarrassed by her own caution. [49]She deliberately chose a route with several points where people could stop without feeling they had failed.[/49] Her training as a physiotherapist helped her explain warm-ups and early warning signs without frightening newcomers. [50]The most difficult session was the first rainy evening, when half the group wanted to cancel and the other half pretended they were not cold.[/50] Aisha shortened the run and ended with hot drinks, which kept everyone returning. [55]She measures progress by conversation, not speed: if runners can talk while moving, they are probably pacing themselves well.[/55] The mixed group has taught her that confidence grows through repeatable successes. She still refuses to call anyone slow, because every participant is faster than the person who stayed home and promised to start next week.
texts.C:
### Liam, The Basketball Referee Mentor
Liam volunteered to mentor teenage basketball referees after noticing how often young officials were criticised by adults during weekend league games. [51]His first rule was that parents could question a decision only after waiting until the end of the quarter.[/51] He had refereed university games for several seasons, but teaching confidence proved different from knowing rules. The teenagers understood fouls; they struggled to keep their whistle hand steady when coaches complained. [52]Liam therefore began filming short practice games so each referee could see their positioning rather than rely on vague comments.[/52] He was surprised by how quickly video soon replaced embarrassment with curiosity. Liam says mentoring officials changed his view of sport, because a fair game depends on people who rarely receive applause, thanks or much patience. He now stands near the loudest adults, not to argue, but to make young referees feel less alone when the hall gets noisy.
texts.D:
### Sofia, The Community Yoga Instructor
Sofia offered free yoga sessions in the community hall after a neighbour lent her mats from a closed gym and helped sweep the floor. [53]She found that the hardest adjustment was avoiding language that assumed everyone could balance, kneel or touch the floor.[/53] Her dance background helped her demonstrate alternatives gracefully and keep movements flowing, rather than presenting them as easier versions. [54]One participant returned after surgery because Sofia had shown a chair-based sequence that allowed her to continue safely.[/54] The old hall was noisy and sometimes cold, but people valued the chance to move without mirrors or competition, especially after stressful days at work. [56]After several weeks, Sofia says the sessions taught her that inclusion is created sentence by sentence.[/56] She now writes every plan with three options for each planned movement, and she asks new volunteers to test instructions with their eyes closed, so unclear directions become obvious.

questions.question: found that sharing playing time fairly was harder than teaching the sport itself?
questions.explanation: Daniel found substitutions harder than teaching passing.

questions.question: used a visible planning tool to reduce complaints from adults watching?
questions.explanation: Daniel's rotation chart reduced touchline arguments.

questions.question: selected a route that allowed participants to pause without embarrassment?
questions.explanation: Aisha chose a route with stopping points that did not feel like failure.

questions.question: adapted a session because bad weather divided the group's motivation?
questions.explanation: Aisha shortened a rainy run when the group reacted differently to the weather.

questions.question: introduced a rule about when adults could challenge young officials?
questions.explanation: Liam made parents wait until the quarter ended before questioning decisions.

questions.question: used recordings to help learners analyse their own positioning?
questions.explanation: Liam filmed practice games so referees could see their positioning.

questions.question: had to change instructions so they did not assume particular physical abilities?
questions.explanation: Sofia avoided language assuming everyone could balance, kneel or touch the floor.

questions.question: mentions a modified routine that helped someone continue after medical treatment?
questions.explanation: Sofia's chair-based sequence helped a participant after surgery.

questions.question: judges improvement by whether people can talk while exercising?
questions.explanation: Aisha measures progress by conversation rather than speed.

questions.question: says careful wording is central to making people feel included?
questions.explanation: Sofia says inclusion is created sentence by sentence.
